key_insight: A new damage-and-fracture constitutive framework is developed to predict failure in additively manufactured polymer TPMS lattices by combining viscoplastic deformation, a polymer damage initiation criterion, and gradient damage evolution.
engineering_relevance: The model enables more reliable in silico design of architected polymer lattices for energy absorption and load-bearing applications where fracture governs performance.
method: Implemented a high-fidelity viscoplastic model in Abaqus/Explicit via UMAT.
Introduced a damage initiation criterion based on stored elastic energy and equivalent plastic strain.
Added a gradient-damage formulation and calibrated/validated it against experiments on TPMS unit cells and lattices.
evidence_signal: The model was quantitatively and qualitatively validated using experimental data from unit cells under non-monotonic loading and lattices under tension.
watch_item: The framework is calibrated for specific polymer TPMS geometries and loading paths, so transferability to other materials, architectures, or rate regimes may require revalidation.
paper_title: Modeling damage and fracture in additively manufactured polymeric triply periodic minimal surface lattices
